شراب.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian شَراب (šarâb), from Arabic شَرَاب (šarāb).

شرارت.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian شَرَارَت (šarārat), borrowed from Arabic شَرَارَة (šarāra), from شَرَّ (šarra).

شرافت.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian شَرَافَت (šarāfat), from Arabic شَرَافَة (šarāfa).

شرط.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian شَرْط (šart), borrowed from Arabic شَرْط (šarṭ). Compare Punjabi ਸ਼ਰਤ (śarat) / شَرْط (śart̤).

شرک.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian شرک (širk), from Arabic شِرْك (širk).

شرکت.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian شِرْکَت (šerkat), from Arabic شِرْكَة (širka).

شرم.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian شَرْم (šarm), from Middle Persian 𐭱𐭥𐭬 (šʿm /⁠šarm⁠/).

شرمگیں.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian شرمگین (śarmagīn). By surface analysis, شَرْم (śarm, “shame”) + گِیں (gī̃).

شروع.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian شُرُوع (šurū'), from Arabic شُرُوع (šurūʕ).

شریر.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian شَرِیر (šarīr), from Arabic شَرِير (šarīr).

شریعت.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian شَرِیعَت (šarī'at), from Arabic شَرِيعَة (šarīʕa). First attested in c. 1421 as Middle Hindi شَرِیعَت (śry't).

شریف.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian شَرِیف (šarīf), from Arabic شَرِيف (šarīf).

شریک سفر.e: From شریک (śarīk, “participant; associate”) + ِ (i, “izafat construction”) + سفر (safr, “journey”), literally “an associate in a journey”.

ششک.e: Learned borrowing from Sanskrit शशक (śaśaka). Doublet of سَسَک (sasak).

ششن.e: Learned borrowing from Sanskrit शिश्न (śiśná), from the root श्नथ् (śnath, “to pierce [a vagina], strike, hurt”).

شطرنج.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian شَطْرَنْج (šatranj), borrowed from Arabic شَطْرَنْج (šaṭranj), borrowed from Classical Persian چَتْرَنگ (čatrang), from Middle Persian 𐭰𐭠𐭲𐭫𐭠𐭭𐭢 (cʾtlʾng /⁠čatrang⁠/, “chess”), borrowed from Sanskrit चतुरङ्ग (caturaṅga, “comprising elephants, chariots, cavalry, and infantry”), from चतुर् (catúr, “four”) + अङ्ग (áṅga, “limb, member”), literally “four limbs; four members”.

شعار.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian شعار (ši'ār), from Arabic شِعَار (šiʕār).

شعاری.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian شعاری (ši'ārī), derived from Arabic شِعَار (šiʕār).

شعرا.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian شعرا (šu'arā), from Arabic شُعَرَاء (šuʕarāʔ).

ex.u: یہ سچ کہ بھڑکتے شعلوں سے دامن کو بچانا کھیل نہیں
ex.e: It is true that to [try and] save a soul from a blazing fire is no easy task

شعلہ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian شُعْلَه (šu'la), borrowed from Arabic شُعْلَة (šuʕla). First attested in c. 1609 as Middle Hindi شعلہ (ś'lh).

شعور.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian شُعُور (šu'ūr), from Arabic شُعُور (šuʕūr). First attested in c. 1635 as Middle Hindi شعور (ś'vr).

senses.g: a male given name from Arabic

شعیب.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian شعیب (šu’ayb), from Arabic شُعَيْب (šuʕayb).

شغل.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian شغل (šuġl), from Arabic شُغْل (šuḡl).

شفا.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian شِفَاء (šifā'), from Arabic شِفَاء (šifāʔ).

شفا خانہ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian شِفَاخَانَه (šifāxāna). By surface analysis, شفا (šifā, “healing”) + خَانَہ (xāna, “area”).

شفقت.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian شَفَقَت (šafaqat), borrowed from Arabic شَفَقَة (šafaqa), from شَفَقَ (šafaqa). First attested in c. 1609 as Middle Hindi شفقت (śfqt).

senses.g: a male given name from Arabic

شقرا.e: Borrowed from Arabic شَقْرَاء (šaqrāʔ), feminine singular of أَشْقَر (ʔašqar).

شک.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian شَک (šak), borrowed from Arabic شَكّ (šakk).

شکار.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian شِکَار (šikār), possibly borrowed from Sogdian 𐽁𐼸𐼰𐽀 (škʾr), from Proto-Iranian *skar-.

شکار گاہ.e: From شِکار (śikār, “hunt; prey”) + گاہ (gāh, “place”), literally “place of the hunt”.

senses.g: Shikarpur (a city in Shikarpur district, Sindh, Pakistan)

شکارپور.e: Corrupted from شَکارِپور (śakāripor), "the town founded by the vanquisher of the Shakas", a name given by the Scythians.

شکایت.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian شِکایَت (šikāyat), from Arabic شِكَايَة (šikāya).

senses.g: a female given name, Shakti, from Sanskrit, of Hindu usage

شکر.e: Learned borrowing from Sanskrit शुक्र (śukra). Doublet of سرخ (surx).
Learned borrowing from Sanskrit शुक्र (śukra). Doublet of سرخ (surx).

شکرانہ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian شُکْرَانَه (šukrāna). By surface analysis, شُکْر (śukr, “thank”) + انَہ, from Arabic شُكْر (šukr).

شکریہ.e: An Indo-Aryan innovation, from شُکْر (śukr) + ـِیَہ (-iya). Compare Bengali শুকরিয়া (śukriẏa), Dhivehi ޝުކުރިއްޔާ (šukuriyyā), Hindi शुक्रिया (śukriyā), Punjabi ਸ਼ੁਕਰੀਆ (śukrīā)/شُکْرِیَہ (śukriyah) (see Arabic شُكْر (šukr) for all cognates). Not borrowed from Arabic شُكْرِيَّة (šukriyya) nor from Persian شکریه (šokriyye), which do not have such meanings.

An Indo-Aryan innovation, from شُکْر (śukr) + ـِیَہ (-iya). Compare Bengali শুকরিয়া (śukriẏa), Dhivehi ޝުކުރިއްޔާ (šukuriyyā), Hindi शुक्रिया (śukriyā), Punjabi ਸ਼ੁਕਰੀਆ (śukrīā)/شُکْرِیَہ (śukriyah) (see Arabic شُكْر (šukr) for all cognates). Not borrowed from Arabic شُكْرِيَّة (šukriyya) nor from Persian شکریه (šokriyye), which do not have such meanings.

شکست.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian شِکَسْت (šikast), from شِکَسْتَن (šikastan), from Middle Persian škstn' (škastan, “to break”).

شکل.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian شَکْل (šakl), borrowed from Arabic شَكْل (šakl).

شکم.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian شِکَم (šikam), from Middle Persian 𐭠𐭱𐭪𐭬𐭡 (aškamb).

senses.g: torment, torture (by a kind of instrument)
inconvenience (or a complaint due to an illness or pain)

شکوہ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian شِکْوَه (šikwa), borrowed from Arabic شَكْوَة (šakwa), from شَكَا (šakā).

senses.g: gift of money or sweets given at an engagement
Silla (an ancient kingdom in modern Korea, one of the Three Kingdoms, existing from 57 BCE to 935 CE; at its maximum covering all of modern South Korea and most of modern North Korea)
Shalya, the legendary king of Madra (in north-central Punjab whose capital was Sialkot) and the Indus in the Mahabharata, who led the Kaurava army against the Pandava usurpers in the east

ex.u: ہمارے کشور کے لیے زیادہ سے زیادہ راجہ شلیہ جیسے مرد ہونے چاہئیں ہے
ex.r: hamāre kiśvar ke liye zyāda se zyāda rāja śalya jaise mard hone cāh'ī̃ he
ex.e: let there be many men like king Shalya for our fatherland

شلیہ.e: Learned borrowing from Sanskrit शल्य (śalya, “spear; javelin; arrow”). Distantly related to Ancient Greek κῆλον (kêlon, “shafts (of arrows)”), Old Prussian kelian (“spear”), Old Norse hali, Albanian thel.

senses.g: North Macedonia (a country on the Balkan Peninsula in Southeast Europe)

شمس.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian شَمْس (šams), borrowed from Arabic شَمْس (šams), from Proto-Semitic *śamš-.

senses.g: a place where corpses are burnt or buried

شمع.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian شَمْع (šam'), from Arabic شَمْع (šamʕ). First attested in c. 1564 as Middle Hindi شَمْع (śm' /⁠śam'⁠/).

senses.g: Shantanu, a mythological king in the mahābharata
a male given name, Shantanu, from Sanskrit

شنتانو.e: Learned borrowing from Sanskrit शंतनु (śáṃtanu). Compare Persian شانتانو (šântânu).

senses.g: Shopian (a city, a district of Jammu and Kashmir, India)

شور شرابہ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian شور شرابه, from شور (śor) + شرابه (śarāba).

شوق.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian شوق (šawq), from Arabic شَوْق (šawq).

senses.g: very strong or powerful, stout, gallant

شہادت.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian شَهَادَت (šahādat), borrowed from Arabic شَهَادَة (šahāda), from شَهِدَ (šahida).

senses.g: a male given name, Shahbaz or Shehbaz, from Persian

شہد.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian شَهْد (šahd), borrowed from Arabic شَهْد (šahd).

senses.g: Shahdadkot (a city in Qambar Shahdadkot district, province of Sindh, Pakistan)

شہر.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian شَهر (šahr), from Middle Persian 𐭱𐭲𐭥𐭩 (štʿy /⁠šahr⁠/), from Proto-Iranian *xšaθram (likely through Avestan 𐬑𐬱𐬀𐬚𐬭𐬀 (xšaθra, “kingdom”)), from Proto-Indo-Iranian *kšatrám, ultimately from Proto-Indo-European *tek- (“to attain”). Cognate with Sanskrit क्षत्र (kṣatrá, “dominion, kingdom”), and Parthian 𐭇𐭔𐭕𐭓 (ḥštr /⁠xšahr⁠/). Doublet of کھاتا (khātā).

شہر سبز.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian شَهْرِ سَبْز (šahr-i sabz, literally “the green city”).

شہرت.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian شُهْرَت (šuhrat), from Arabic شُهْرَة (šuhra). First attested in c. 1688 as Middle Hindi شہرت (śhrt).

شہرستان.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian شهرستان (šahristān). By surface analysis, شَہْر (śahr) + ـِسْتان (-istān).

senses.g: a male given name, Shahroz, Shahroze, or Shehroze, from Persian
prince (a king's son, grandson or other male descendant)

شہزادہ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian شهزاده (šahzāda) or شاهزاده (šāhzāda, “born of king”).

senses.g: princess (a king's daughter, granddaughter or other female descendant)

شہزادی.e: From شَہْزادَہ (śahzāda) + ـی (-ī). First attested in 1784 as Middle Hindi شاہزادی (śahzady /⁠śāhzādī⁠/).

senses.g: having a tinge of blue or gray, or of red, or green, in the black
having dark gray eyes with a shade of red

شہلا.e: Borrowed from Arabic شَهْلَاء (šahlāʔ), feminine singular of أَشْهَل (ʔašhal).

senses.g: a female given name, Shahla, from Arabic

شہلا.e: Borrowed from Arabic شَهْلَاء (šahlāʔ), feminine singular of أَشْهَل (ʔašhal).

شہمات.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian شَهْمَات (šahmāt), شَاهمَات (šāh-māt) or شَاه مَات (šāh māt, literally “the king is amazed”). Cognate with English checkmate.

شہنشاہ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian شَهَنْشَاه (šahanšāh) or شَاهَنْشَاه (šāhanšāh, “king of kings”).

senses.g: demolished, destroyed (of a minaret, mosque etc.)

شہید.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian شَهِید (šahīd), borrowed from Arabic شَهِيد (šahīd), from شَهِدَ (šahida).

senses.g: a witness; one who bears witness

شہید.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian شَهِید (šahīd), borrowed from Arabic شَهِيد (šahīd), from شَهِدَ (šahida).

شیخ.e: From Classical Persian شَیْخ (šayx), from Arabic شَيْخ (šayḵ).

senses.g: a sheik (and all the meaning that encompass, inc. but not limited to) — a leader of an Arab tribe
a sheik (and all the meaning that encompass, inc. but not limited to) — an Islamic cleric

شیخ.e: From Classical Persian شَیْخ (šayx), from Arabic شَيْخ (šayḵ).

senses.g: a male caste surname originating as a patronymic, equivalent to English Sheikh

شیخ.e: From Classical Persian شَیْخ (šayx), from Arabic شَيْخ (šayḵ).

شیدا.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian شَیدَا (šaydā, “mad, insane; enamored”), from Aramaic שידא (šēḏā) or Classical Syriac ܫܐܕܐ (šēḏā, “demon”), from Akkadian 𒀭𒆘 (šēdum, “a protective deity”).

ex.u: میری شیدا نے مجھے ہونٹوں پر بوسہ دیا.
ex.r: merī śaidā ne mujhe honṭõ par bosa diyā.
ex.e: My lover kissed me on the lips.

شیدا.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian شَیدَا (šaydā, “mad, insane; enamored”), from Aramaic שידא (šēḏā) or Classical Syriac ܫܐܕܐ (šēḏā, “demon”), from Akkadian 𒀭𒆘 (šēdum, “a protective deity”).

شیر آبی.e: From Classical Persian شیر (šēr, “lion”) + آبی (ābī, “of the water”) (literally, water lion).

senses.g: a female given name, Shirin, from Persian
a female given name, Shirin, from Persian — Shirin (Queen of Armenia and wife of Khosrow II, a character in the Shahnameh and in Khosrow and Shirin)
Shesha, a serpentine demigod and king of the serpents.
vocative plural of شِیشَہ (śīśa, “glass; pane; window”)
oblique plural of شِیشَہ (śīśa, “glass; pane; window”)

شیشہ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian شیشه (šīša), from Middle Persian. First attested in c. 1564 as Middle Hindi شیشہ (śyśh).

شیشی.e: Diminutive of شیشہ (šīša), from Classical Persian شیشه (šīša).

senses.g: inflection of شِیشَہ (śīśa, “glass; pane; window”): — direct plural
inflection of شِیشَہ (śīśa, “glass; pane; window”): — oblique/vocative singular

شیطان.e: Borrowed from Arabic شَيْطَان (šayṭān), likely through Classical Persian شَیْطان (šaytān).
Borrowed from Arabic شَيْطَان (šayṭān), likely through Classical Persian شَیْطان (šaytān).
Borrowed from Arabic شَيْطَان (šayṭān), likely through Classical Persian شَیْطان (šaytān).

شیطنت.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian شیطنت (šaytanat), from Arabic شَيْطَنَة (šayṭana), from the verb شَيْطَنَ (šayṭana, “to act as the devil”).

senses.g: Sheffield (a city and metropolitan borough of South Yorkshire, England, United Kingdom)
a historical region of Rajasthan, India
Shina or Shinas: a Dardic people.

شینا.e: Borrowed from Shina ݜݨیاٗ (ṣiṇyaá), from Sanskrit श्रेण्यक (śreṇyaka), from श्रेण्य (śreṇya, “tribesman”).

senses.g: Sheikhupura (a city in Sheikhupura district, Punjab, Pakistan)
The twentieth letter of the Urdu alphabet, written in the Arabic script; preceded by ش and followed by ض.
washing powder (ie. for a washing machine etc.)
Sabian (member of Sabianism, an unknown religion mentioned in the Qur'an)

ex.u: یَقِیناً جو لوگ اِیمَان لَائے اَور جو یَہُودی ہوگِئے اَور نَصْرَانِی اَور صَابی جو کوئی بھی اِیمَان لَایَا (اُن میں سے) اَللّٰہ پَر اَور یَومِ آخِر پَر اَور اُس نے اَچّھے عَمَل کِیے تو اُن کے لِیے (مَحْفُوظ) ہَے اُن کَا اَجَر اُن کے رُب کے پَاس اَور نَہ اُن پَر کوئی خَوف ہوگَا اَور نَہ غَمْگِین ہوں گے۔
ex.r: yaqīnan jo log īmān lāe aur jo yahūdī hogie aur nasrānī aur sābī jo koī bhī īmān lāyā (un mẽ se) allāh par aur yaum-i āxir par aur us ne acche 'amal kiye to un ke liye (mahfūz) hai un kā ajar un ke rub ke pās aur na un par koī xauf hogā aur na ġamgīn hõ ge.
ex.e: Indeed, the believers, Jews, Christians, and Sabians—whoever ˹truly˺ believes in Allah and the Last Day and does good will have their reward with their Lord. And there will be no fear for them, nor will they grieve.

صاحب.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian صَاحِب (sāhib), from Arabic صَاحِب (ṣāḥib).

senses.g: son of a noble, son of a lord

صاحبزادہ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian صَاحِبزادَه. By surface analysis, صاحب (sāhab, “lord”) + زادہ (-zāda).

senses.g: Sadiqabad (a city in Rahim Yar Khan district, Punjab, Pakistan)

صادق آباد.e: From صادِق (sādiq, “Sadeq Mohammad Khan V”) + آباد (ābād).

صارف.e: From صرف (ṣarf), borrowed from Arabic صَرْف (ṣarf).

صاف.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian صَاف (sāf), from Arabic صَافٍ (ṣāfin).

صالح.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian صَالِح (sālih), from Arabic صَالِح (ṣāliḥ).

senses.g: a male given name from Arabic

صالح.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian صَالِح (sālih), from Arabic صَالِح (ṣāliḥ).

senses.g: right, going straight to the point
well-aimed, to the point, right sound in judgement

صائب.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian صائب (sā'ib), from Arabic صَائِب (ṣāʔib).

صبح.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian صُبْح (subh), from Arabic صُبْح (ṣubḥ).
Borrowed from Classical Persian صُبْح (subh), from Arabic صُبْح (ṣubḥ).

صبح بخیر.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian صُبْح بَخَیْر (subh baxayr).

صبر.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian صَبْر (sabr), borrowed from Arabic صَبْر (ṣabr). Compare Bengali সবর (śobor).

senses.g: good things come to those who wait

صبر کا پھل میٹھا ہوتا ہے.r: ṣabr kā phal mīṭhā hotā hai
صبر کا پھل میٹھا ہوتا ہے.e: Literally, “The fruit of patience is [always] sweet.”

صحبت.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian صُحْبَت (suhbat), from Arabic صُحْبَة (ṣuḥba).

senses.g: Sohbatpur (a city in Sohbatpur district, Balochistan, Pakistan)

ex.u: شَراب نوشی صِحَت کے لِیے مُضِر ہَے۔
ex.r: śarāb nośī sihat ke liye muzir hai.

صحت.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian صِحَّت (sihhat), from Arabic صِحَّة (ṣiḥḥa).

senses.g: a place with medical care for sick or injured people, a hospital or infirmary

صحرا.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian صَحْرَا (sahrā), borrowed from Arabic صَحْرَاء (ṣaḥrāʔ).

صحرائے اعظم.e: Literally, “the greatest desert”. Compare Arabic الصَّحْرَاء الْكُبْرَى (aṣ-ṣaḥrāʔ al-kubrā).

صحیح.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian صَحِیح (sahīh), from Arabic صَحِيح (ṣaḥīḥ).

senses.g: book, writing, letter, epistle revealed to a divinely ordained prophet

صحیفہ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian صَحِیفَه (sahīfa), from Arabic صَحِيفَة (ṣaḥīfa).

صحیفہ خواں.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian صحیفہ and خوان.

صد.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian صد (sad) from Old Persian *θata-, from Proto-Iranian *catám, from Proto-Indo-Iranian *ćatám, from Proto-Indo-European *ḱm̥tóm. Compare Sanskrit शत (śatá).

صدا.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian صدا (sadā), from Arabic صَدًى (ṣadan).

صداقت.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian صَدَاقَت (sadāqat), from Arabic صَدَاقَة (ṣadāqa).

senses.g: chief, head; host (of an event etc.)

صدر.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian صَدْر (sadr), from Arabic صَدْر (ṣadr).

senses.g: Saddar (a neighbourhood of Karachi, Sindh, Pakistan)

صدر.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian صَدْر (sadr), from Arabic صَدْر (ṣadr).

صدر مملکت.e: Ezafe compound of صدر (sadar, “chief, head”) + مملکت (mumlikat, “dominion”), literally “chief or head of the dominion”.

senses.g: A form of charity to please Allah.

صدی.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian صدی (sadī, “a hundred”).

ex.u: مجھے صرف دو اور روپے کی ضرورت ہے۔
ex.r: mujhe sirf do aur rupe kī zarūrat hai.
ex.e: I only need two more rupees.

صرف.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian صِرْف (sirf), from Arabic صِرْف (ṣirf).
Borrowed from Classical Persian صِرْف (sirf), from Arabic صِرْف (ṣirf).
Borrowed from Classical Persian صَرْف (sarf), from Arabic صَرْف (ṣarf).
Borrowed from Classical Persian صَرْف (sarf), from Arabic صَرْف (ṣarf).

senses.g: difficult, hard, arduous, troublesome, hard to be borne or endured, disagreeable, distressing

صغیر.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian صغیر (saġīr), from Arabic صَغِير (ṣaḡīr).

صفت.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian صِفَت (sifat), from Arabic صِفَة (ṣifa).

صفحہ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian صَفْحَه (safha), borrowed from Arabic صَفْحَة (ṣafḥa).

صفحۂ اول.e: From صفحہ (ṣafḥah, “page”) + ء (“pertaining to a”, hamza, iẓāfat construction) + اول (avval, “first”).

صفر.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian صِفْر (sifr), from Arabic صِفْر (ṣifr), a semantic loan from Sanskrit शू॒न्य (śūnyá, “empty; void”), whence also سُونا (sūnā). Doublet of زِیرو (zīro).
Borrowed from Classical Persian صِفْر (sifr), from Arabic صِفْر (ṣifr), a semantic loan from Sanskrit शू॒न्य (śūnyá, “empty; void”), whence also سُونا (sūnā). Doublet of زِیرو (zīro).

صفرا.e: Borrowed from Arabic صَفْرَاء (ṣafrāʔ), feminine singular of أَصْفَر (ʔaṣfar).
Borrowed from Arabic صَفْرَاء (ṣafrāʔ), feminine singular of أَصْفَر (ʔaṣfar).

صقلیہ.e: Borrowed from Arabic صِقِلِّيَّة (ṣiqilliyya), from Ancient Greek Σικελία (Sikelía).

senses.g: capability; capacity (to do or carry out something)
a good characteristic (of someone or something)
a daily logbook of accounts and passengers, by the police

صلاحیت.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian صَلَاحِیَّت (salāhiyyat), from Arabic صَلَاحِيَّة (ṣalāḥiyya). By surface analysis, صَلاح (salāh) + ـِیَت (-iyat).

senses.g: only used in صلیٰ اللہ علیہ وسلم (sallā-llāhu 'alayhi wa-sallam)

صلی.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian صَلَّیٰ (sallā), from Arabic صَلَّى (ṣallā, “he blessed; may he bless”).

senses.g: God shower blessing and well-being upon him and grant him peace

صلی اللہ علیہ وسلم.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian صَلَّیٰ ٱللّٰهُ عَلَیْهِ وَسَلَّم (sallā l-lāhu alayh-i wasallam), from Arabic صَلَّى اللهُ عَلَيْهِ وَسَلَّمَ (ṣallā llāhu ʕalayhi wa-sallama).

صلیب.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian صَلِیب (salīb), borrowed from Arabic صَلِيب (ṣalīb), borrowed from Aramaic צְלִיבָא (ṣəlīḇā), borrowed from Akkadian 𒁇 (ṣalāpu, “to cross out, to pervert; to give difficulty or be crooked, not be easy or straight”).

صندوق.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian صَنْدُوق (sandūq), borrowed from Arabic صَنْدُوق (ṣandūq), borrowed from Aramaic צנדוק (ṣəndūq, “coffer, especially one storing documents, safe-guarding precious items”), borrowed from Ancient Greek σῠνθήκη (sŭnthḗkē), from σῠντῐ́θημῐ (sŭntĭ́thēmĭ, “to place or put together”), from συν- (sun-) + τίθημι (títhēmi).

صندوقچہ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian صندوقچه (sandūqča), diminutive of صندوق (sandūq), from Arabic صُنْدُوق (ṣundūq).

صنعت.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian صَنْعَت (san'at), from Arabic صَنْعَة (ṣanʕa).

صنف.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian صِنْف (sinf), from Arabic صِنْف (ṣinf).

صنم.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian صَنَم (sanam), from Arabic صَنَم (ṣanam).

صنوبر.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian صَنَوْبَر (sanawbar), from Arabic صَنَوْبَر (ṣanawbar).

senses.g: Swabi (a city in Swabi district, Khyber Pakhtunkhwa, Pakistan)

صورت.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian صُورَت (sūrat), borrowed from Arabic صُورَة (ṣūra). First attested in c. 1421 as Middle Hindi صورت (ṣvrt /⁠sūrat⁠/).

صورت حال.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian صورت حال (sūrat-i-hāl).

صوفہ.e: Borrowed from English sofa. Spelling influenced by Arabic صُفَّة (ṣuffa).

senses.g: Somalia (a country in East Africa, in the Horn of Africa)

صومالیہ.e: Borrowed from English Somalia. Spelling affected by Arabic صُومَال (ṣūmāl).

senses.g: The twenty-first letter of the Urdu alphabet, written in the Arabic script; preceded by ص and followed by ط.

ضابطہ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian ضَابِطَه (zābita), from Arabic ضَابِطَة (ḍābiṭa).

ضائع.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian ضَایِع (zāyi'), borrowed from Arabic ضَائِع (ḍāʔiʕ), from ضَاعَ (ḍāʕa). First attested in c. 1609 as Middle Hindi ضایع (ẓay'). Compare Punjabi ضائَع (ẓāyaʻ) / ਜ਼ਾਇਆ (zāiā).

ضبط.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian ضَبْط (zabt), borrowed from Arabic ضَبْط (ḍabṭ).
Borrowed from Classical Persian ضَبْط (zabt), borrowed from Arabic ضَبْط (ḍabṭ).

ex.u: موت کے بعد انسان کی تقدیر کے بارے میں بدھ مت کا جو تصور تھا وہ برہمی نظریوں کی عین ضد تھا
ex.e: The Buddhist concept of the destiny of man after death was completely opposite to Brahmanist theories.

ضد.e: First attested in c. 1665 as Middle Hindi ضد (zid, zidd), borrowed from Classical Persian ضِدّ (zidd), itself from Arabic ضِدّ (ḍidd). Cognate with Punjabi ضِدّ (ẓidd).

ex.u: انھوں نے بڑے اصرار کے ساتھ مجھ سے کہا کہ "ضِد چھوڑ دو۔ اِس معمولی بات پر معاملات کو بگڑنے نہ دو۔"
ex.e: He insistently told me "Leave this stubbornness of yours. Don't let affairs deteriorate over this trivial matter."

ضد.e: First attested in c. 1665 as Middle Hindi ضد (zid, zidd), borrowed from Classical Persian ضِدّ (zidd), itself from Arabic ضِدّ (ḍidd). Cognate with Punjabi ضِدّ (ẓidd).

ضرر.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian ضرر (zarar), from Arabic ضَرَر (ḍarar).

ضرور.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian ضرور (zurūr), from Arabic ضُرُور (ḍurūr).
Borrowed from Classical Persian ضرور (zurūr), from Arabic ضُرُور (ḍurūr).

ضروری.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian ضروری (ẓarūrī), itself from Arabic ضَرُورِيّ (ḍarūriyy). By surface analysis, ضرور (ẓurūr) + ی (ī).

ضعیف.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian ضَعِیف (za'īf), borrowed from Arabic ضَعِيف (ḍaʕīf), from ضَعُفَ (ḍaʕufa).

senses.g: a weak; old; poor (person or man)

ضعیف.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian ضَعِیف (za'īf), borrowed from Arabic ضَعِيف (ḍaʕīf), from ضَعُفَ (ḍaʕufa).
